w:r.w:t: them, even if their signs of illness are the same as yours.
possible side effects not listed in this leaflet. See section 4.

w:bookmarkStart.w:name: 1. What Invokana is and what it is used

w:r.w:t: This medicine works by increasing the amount of sugar removed from your body in your urine. This reduces the amount of sugar in your blood and can help prevent heart disease in patients with type 2 diabetes
with T2DM by a mechanism beyond blood glucose lowering.
Invokana can be used by itself or along with other medicines you may be using to treat your type 2 diabetes
lower blood sugar levels. You may already be taking one or more of these to treat your type 2 diabetes.
that your body produces does not work as well as it should. Your body can also make too much sugar.
conditions such as heart disease, kidney disease, blindness, and amputation.

w:bookmarkStart.w:name: 2. What you need to know before you take

w:r.w:t: if you experience rapid weight loss, feeling sick or being sick, stomach pain, excessive thirst, fast

breath, a sweet or metallic taste in your mouth or a different odour to your urine or sweat, talk to a doctor or go to the nearest hospital immediately. These symptoms could be a sign of “diabetic ketoacidosis” –

with diabetes because of increased levels of “ketone bodies” in your urine or blood, seen in tests. The risk of developing diabetic ketoacidosis may be increased with prolonged fasting, excessive alcohol consumption, dehydration, sudden reductions in insulin dose, or a higher need of insulin due to major surgery or serious illness.
your doctor if you need to stop taking Invokana and when to start it again.
rapid weight loss, nausea, or vomiting). Invokana should not be used to treat this condition.
low blood pressure (hypotension). More information is given below in “Other medicines and
It is important to check your feet regularly and adhere to any other advice regarding foot care and adequate hydration given by your healthcare professional. You should notify your doctor immediately
or pain in your feet. Some studies indicate that taking canagliflozin may have contributed to the risk of lower limb amputation (mainly toe and midfoot amputations).
tenderness, redness, or swelling of the genitals or the area between the genitals and the anus with fever or feeling generally unwell. These symptoms could be a sign of a rare but serious or even
ask you to stop taking Invokana until you have recovered.
below 10 years of age. Invokana is not recommended for children under 10 years.
Tell your doctor or pharmacist if you are taking, have recently taken, or might take any other medicines.
some other medicines can affect the way this medicine works.
medicines used to lower your blood pressure (anti-hypertensives), including diuretics
since this medicine can also lower your blood pressure by removing levels of excess water in the body. Possible signs of losing too much fluid from your body are listed in section 4.
or digitoxin in your blood may need to be checked if taken with Invokana.

If you are pregnant or breast-feeding, think you may be pregnant or are planning to have a baby, ask your doctor or pharmacist for advice before taking or continuing to take this medicine. Invokana should
Invokana and control your blood sugar as soon as you know that you are pregnant.
stop taking this medicine or to stop breast-feeding.
Invokana has no or negligible influence on the ability to drive, cycle, and use tools or machines. However,
drive, cycle, or use tools or machines.

Taking Invokana with other medicines for diabetes called sulphonylureas (such as glimepiride or glipizide) or insulin can increase the risk of having low blood sugar (hypoglycaemia). Signs include blurred
or confused. This may affect your ability to drive, cycle, and use any tools or machines. Tell your doctor as soon as possible if you get any of the signs of low blood sugar.
doctor or pharmacist if you are not sure.
to increase your dose to 300 mg.
If your doctor has prescribed canagliflozin along with any bile acid sequestrant such as cholestyramine
least 1 hour before or 4 hours to 6 hours after the bile acid sequestrant.
to take all medicines as directed by your doctor to achieve the best results for your health.
doctor, pharmacist or nurse. In particular, if you are following a diabetic weight control diet, continue to follow it while you are taking this medicine.
for the next dose, skip the missed dose.
medicine without talking to your doctor first.
have any of the following serious side effects:
as the kidney function gets worse. The doctor may decide to temporarily or permanently stop the treatment with Invokana.
people (aged 75 and over), people with kidney problems, and people taking water tablets (diuretics). Possible signs of dehydration are:
effects: Hypoglycaemia (very common, may affect more than 1 in 10 people)
a sulphonylurea (such as glimepiride or glipizide).
to urinate, need to urinate at night)
red blood cells in your blood (haematocrit).
the genitals or the area between the genitals and the anus.

If you get any side effects, talk to your doctor, pharmacist or nurse. This includes any possible side effects not listed in this leaflet. You can also report side effects directly via the Yellow Card Scheme at
The expiry date refers to the last day of that month.
to throw away medicines you no longer use. These measures will help protect the environment.

w:bookmarkStart.w:name: 6. Contents of the pack and other inform

w:r.w:t: cellulose (E460[i]), hydroxypropyl cellulose (E463), croscarmellose sodium (E468) and magnesium stearate (E572).
film-coating: polyvinyl alcohol (E1203), titanium dioxide (E171), macrogol/PEG 3350
with “CFZ” on one side and “100” on the other side.
with “CFZ” on one side and “300” on the other side.
of 10 x 1, 30 x 1, 90 x 1, or 100 x 1 tablets.
Janssen-Cilag SpA Via C. Janssen Borgo
